speaker: Unknown
en: You guys hit a billion in revenue in less than four years with around 60 to 70 people.
zh: 你们在不到四年的时间里,收入达到了十亿,人员只有 60 到 70 人左右。

en: You're completely bootstrapped, haven't raised any VC money.
zh: 你们完全是自力更生的,没有筹集任何 VC 资金。

speaker: Unknown
en: I don't believe anyone has ever done this before.

speaker: Unknown
en: We basically never wanted to play the Silicon Valley game.
zh: 我们基本上从来不想玩硅谷的游戏。

speaker: Unknown
en: I always thought it was ridiculous.
zh: 我一直认为这很荒谬。

speaker: Unknown
en: I used to work at a bunch of the big tech companies and I always felt that we could fire 90% of the people and we would move faster because the best people wouldn't have all these distractions.
zh: 我曾在一些大型科技公司工作,我总觉得我们可以解雇 90% 的人,我们会走得更快,因为最优秀的人不会有这些干扰。

speaker: Unknown
en: So when we start Surge, we wanted to build it completely differently with a super small, super elite team.
zh: 所以当我们开始创办 Surge 时,我们想用一个非常小、非常精锐的团队来完全不同地构建它。

speaker: Unknown
en: You guys are by far the most successful data company out there.
zh: 你们是迄今为止最成功的数据公司。

speaker: Unknown
en: We essentially teach AI models what's good and what's bad.
zh: 我们本质上是教 AI 模型什么是好的,什么是坏的。

speaker: Unknown
en: People don't understand what quality even means in this space.
zh: 人们在这个领域甚至不理解质量意味着什么。

speaker: Unknown
en: They think you could just throw bodies at a problem and get good data, that's completely wrong.
zh: 他们认为你可以只是把人力投向一个问题并获得好的数据,这完全是错的。

speaker: Unknown
en: To a regular person, it doesn't feel like these models are getting that much smarter constantly.
zh: 对于普通人来说,感觉这些模型并没有持续变得那么聪明。

speaker: Unknown
en: Over the past year, I've realized that the values that the companies have will shape the model.
zh: 在过去的一年里,我意识到公司的价值观将塑造模型。

speaker: Unknown
en: I was asking Claude to help me drop an email the other day.
zh: 有一天我让 Claude 帮我写一封邮件。

speaker: Unknown
en: And after 30 minutes, yeah, I think it really crafted me the perfect email and I sent it.
zh: 30 分钟后,是的,我觉得它真的为我草拟了一封完美的邮件,然后我发了出去。

speaker: Unknown
en: But then I realized that I spent 30 minutes doing something that didn't matter at all.
zh: 但后来我意识到,我花了 30 分钟做一件完全无关紧要的事情。

speaker: Unknown
en: If you could choose the perfect model behavior, which model would you want?
zh: 如果你能选择完美的模型行为,你会想要哪个模型?

speaker: Unknown
en: Do you want a model that says, "You're absolutely right.
zh: 你想要一个说“你绝对没错。

speaker: Unknown
en: There are definitely 20 more ways to improve this email," and it continues for 50 more iterations or do you want a model that's optimizing for your time and productivity and just says, "No.
zh: 肯定还有 20 种方法可以改进这封邮件”,然后继续迭代 50 次的模型,还是想要一个为你节省时间和生产力并只说“不。

speaker: Unknown
en: You need to stop.
zh: 你需要停下来。

speaker: Unknown
en: Your email's great.
zh: 你的邮件很棒。

speaker: Unknown
en: Just send it and move on"?
zh: 直接发送并继续”的模型?

speaker: Unknown
en: You have this hot take that a lot of these labs are pushing AGI in the wrong direction.
zh: 你有一个很激烈的观点,认为许多实验室正在错误的方向推动 AGI。

speaker: Unknown
en: I'm worried that instead of building AI that will actually advance us as a species, curing cancer, solving poverty, understand the universe, we are optimizing for AI slop instead.
zh: 我担心我们没有建立能够真正推动我们物种进步、治愈癌症、解决贫困、理解宇宙的 AI,而是在为 AI 垃圾进行优化。

speaker: Unknown
en: But we're optimizing your models for the types of people who buy tabloids at a grocery store.
zh: 但我们正在为那些在杂货店买小报的人优化你的模型。

speaker: Unknown
en: We're basically teaching our models to chase dopamine instead of truth.
zh: 我们基本上是在教我们的模型追求多巴胺而不是真理。

speaker: Unknown
en: Today, my guest is Edwin Chan, founder and CEO of Surge AI.
zh: 今天,我的嘉宾是 Edwin Chan,Surge AI 的创始人兼 CEO。

speaker: Unknown
en: Edwin is an extraordinary CEO and Surge is an extraordinary company.
zh: Edwin 是一位非凡的 CEO,Surge 是一家非凡的公司。

speaker: Unknown
en: They're the leading AI data company, powering training at every frontier AI lab.
zh: 它们是领先的 AI 数据公司,为每个前沿 AI 实验室的训练提供动力。

speaker: Unknown
en: They're also the fastest company to ever hit $1 billion in revenue in just four years after launch with fewer than 100 people and also completely bootstrapped.
zh: 它们也是有史以来收入增长最快的公司,在推出后仅四年内就达到了 10 亿美元的收入,员工少于 100 人,且完全是自力更生的。

speaker: Unknown

speaker: Unknown

zh: 正如你将在这次对话中听到的,Edwin 对于如何构建一家重要的公司,以及如何构建对人类真正有益和有用的 AI 有着非常不同的看法。

speaker: Unknown
en: I absolutely love this conversation and I learned a ton.
zh: 我绝对喜欢这次对话,我也学到了很多。

speaker: Unknown
en: I'm really excited for you to hear it.

speaker: Unknown
en: If you enjoy this podcast, don't forget to subscribe and follow it in your favorite podcasting app or YouTube.

speaker: Unknown
en: It helps tremendously.

en: My podcast guests tonight love talking about craft, and taste, and agency, and product market fit.

en: You know what we don't love talking about?

en: SOC 2.

en: Establishing trust isn't optional.

en: Vanta makes it automatic.

en: Get $1,000 off at vanta.com/lenny.

zh: OpenAI、Cursor、Perplexity、Vercel、Plad 和数百家其他获胜的公司有什么共同点？

zh: 如果你在为企业构建软件，你可能会感受到集成单点登录、Skim、RBAC、审计日志以及大客户所需的其他功能的痛苦。

en: Whether you're a seed stage startup trying to land your first enterprise customer or a unicorn expanding globally, WorkOS is the fastest path to becoming enterprise-ready and unlocking growth.
zh: 无论你是试图达成第一个企业客户的种子轮初创公司，还是正在全球扩张的独角兽企业，WorkOS 都是成为企业就绪并解锁增长的最快路径。

en: They're essentially Stripe for enterprise features.

en: Visit workos.com to get started or just hit up their Slack support where they have real engineers in there who answer your questions superfast.

speaker: Unknown
en: WorkOS allows you to build like the best with delightful APIs, comprehensive docs, and a smooth developer experience.
zh: WorkOS 允许你像最优秀的团队一样构建，拥有令人愉悦的 API、全面的文档和顺畅的开发者体验。

speaker: Unknown
en: Go to workos.com to make your app enterprise ready today.
zh: 前往 workos.com 今天让你的应用实现企业就绪。

speaker: Unknown
en: Edwin, thank you so much for being here and welcome to the podcast.
zh: Edwin，非常感谢你能来到这里，欢迎参加播客。

speaker: Unknown
en: Thanks so much for having me.
zh: 非常感谢你的邀请。

speaker: Unknown
en: I'm super excited.
zh: 我超级兴奋。

speaker: Unknown
en: I want to start with just how absurd what you've achieved is.
zh: 我想先从你所取得成就的荒谬程度开始。

speaker: Unknown
en: A lot of people and a lot of companies talk about scaling massive businesses with very few people as a result of AI, and you guys have done this in a way that is unprecedented.
zh: 很多人很多公司都在谈论因为 AI 而利用很少的人来大规模扩展业务，而你们以一种前所未有的方式做到了这一点。

speaker: Unknown
en: You guys hit a billion in revenue in less than four years with less than 60, around 60 to 70 people, you're completely bootstrapped, haven't raised any VC money, I don't believe anyone has ever done this before, so you guys are actually achieving the dream of what people are describing will happen with AI.
zh: 你们在不到四年的时间里实现了 10 亿的收入，员工人数不到 60 人，大约 60 到 70 人，你们完全是自力更生的，没有筹集任何 VC 资金，我不相信以前有人做过这个，所以你们实际上正在实现人们描述 AI 将带来的梦想。

speaker: Unknown
en: I'm curious just, do you think this will happen more and more as a result of AI?
zh: 我只是好奇，你认为随着 AI 的发展这种情况会越来越多吗？

speaker: Unknown
en: And also just where has AI most helped you find leverage to be able to do this?
zh: 还有就是，AI 最主要是在哪些方面帮助你们找到了实现这一点的杠杆作用？

speaker: Unknown
en: Yeah, so we hit over a billion of revenue last year with under 100 people.
zh: 是的，所以我们在去年实现了超过 10 亿的收入，员工人数不到 100 人。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I think we're going to see companies with even crazier ratios, like 100 billion per employee in the next few years.
zh: 而且我认为我们会看到拥有更疯狂比例的公司，比如未来几年每位员工 1000 亿美元。

speaker: Unknown
en: AI is just going to get better and better and make things more efficient so that ratio just becomes inevitable.
zh: AI 只会变得越来越好，并提高效率，所以这个比例是不可避免的。

speaker: Unknown
en: I used to work at a bunch of the big tech companies and I always felt that we could fire 90% of people and we would move faster because the best people wouldn't have all these distractions.
zh: 我曾经在一些大型科技公司工作，我总是觉得我们可以解雇 90% 的人，我们会走得更快，因为最优秀的人不会有所有这些干扰。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so when we started Surge, we wanted to build it completely differently with a super small, super elite team, and yeah, what's crazy is that we actually succeeded.
zh: 所以当我们开始 Surge 时，我们想用一种完全不同的方式来构建它，使用一个超级小、超级精英的团队，是的，疯狂的是我们实际上成功了。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so I think two things are colliding.
zh: 所以我认为两件事正在碰撞。

speaker: Unknown
en: One is that people are realizing that you don't have to build giant organizations in order to win.
zh: 一是人们意识到为了获胜你不必建立庞大的组织。

speaker: Unknown
en: And two, yeah, all these efficiencies from AI.
zh: 二是，是的，AI 带来的所有这些效率。

speaker: Unknown
en: And they're just going to lead to a really amazing time in company building.
zh: 它们将导致公司建设的一个非常惊人的时期。

speaker: Unknown
en: The thing I'm excited about is that the types of companies are going to change too.
zh: 我感到兴奋的是，公司的类型也将发生变化。

speaker: Unknown
en: It won't just be that they're smaller, we're going to see fundamentally different companies emerging.
zh: 不仅仅是它们会变小，我们将看到根本不同的公司出现。

speaker: Unknown
en: If you think about it, fewer employees means less capital.
zh: 想一想，更少的员工意味着更少的资本。

speaker: Unknown
en: Less capital means you don't need a raise.
zh: 更少的资本意味着你不需要融资。

speaker: Unknown
en: So instead of companies started by founders who are great at pitching and great at hyping, you'll get founders who are really great at technology and product.
zh: 因此，不是由那些擅长推销和炒作的创始人创立的公司，你将得到真正擅长技术和产品的创始人。

speaker: Unknown
en: And instead of products optimized for revenue and what VCs want to see, you'll get more interesting ones built by these tiny obsessed teams.
zh: 不是为收入和 VC 想看的东西而优化的产品，你将得到这些微小且痴迷的团队构建的更有趣的产品。

speaker: Unknown
en: So people building things they actually care about, real technology and real innovation.
zh: 所以人们构建他们真正关心的东西，真正的技术和真正的创新。

speaker: Unknown
en: So I'm actually really hoping that the slick on [inaudible 00:07:06], it'll go back to being updates for hackers again.
zh: 所以我真的希望那些光鲜亮丽的[难以辨认 00:07:06]，它将回归为黑客的更新。

speaker: Unknown
en: You guys have done a lot of things in a very contrarian way, and one was actually just not being on LinkedIn, posting viral posts, not on Twitter, constantly promoting Surge.
zh: 你们以非常逆向的方式做了很多事情，其中一个实际上是不在 LinkedIn 上，不发布病毒式传播的帖子，不在 Twitter 上，不断推广 Surge。

speaker: Unknown
en: I think most people hadn't heard of Surge until just recently, and then you just came out, and like, okay, the fastest growing company at a billion dollars.
zh: 我想大多数人在直到最近之前都没听说过 Surge，然后你们就这样出来了，就像，好吧，十亿美元增长最快的公司。

speaker: Unknown
en: Why would you do that?
zh: 你为什么要那样做？

speaker: Unknown
en: I imagine that was very intentional.
zh: 我想那是非常有意的。

speaker: Unknown
en: We basically never wanted to play the Silicon Valley game.
zh: 我们基本上从来不想玩硅谷的游戏。

speaker: Unknown
en: And like I always thought it was ridiculous.
zh: 就像我总是觉得那很荒谬。

speaker: Unknown
en: What did you dream of doing when you were a kid?
zh: 你小时候梦想做什么？

speaker: Unknown
en: Was it building a company from scratch yourself and getting in the weeds of your code and your product every day?
zh: 是自己从头开始建立一家公司，每天深入你的代码和产品的细节吗？

speaker: Unknown
en: Or was it explaining all your decisions to VCs and getting on this giant PR and fundraising hamster wheel?
zh: 还是向 VC 解释你所有的决定，并陷入这个巨大的 PR 和融资的仓鼠轮中？

speaker: Unknown
en: And it definitely made things more difficult for us, because yeah, when you fundraise, you just naturally get part of this kind of Silicon Valley industrial complex where people will, your VCs will tweet about you.
zh: 这绝对让我们的事情变得更困难了，因为是的，当你融资时，你自然会成为这个硅谷综合企业的一部分，人们会，你的 VC 会发推文谈论你。

speaker: Unknown
en: You'll get the tech runs outlines, you'll get announced in all of the newspapers because you raised at this massive valuation.
zh: 你会得到科技媒体的报道，你会在所有报纸上被宣布，因为你以这个巨大的估值筹集了资金。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so it made things more difficult us because the only way we were going to succeed was by building a 10 times better product and getting word of mouth from researchers.
zh: 所以这让我们的事情变得更困难，因为我们成功的唯一方式是构建一个 10 倍好的产品，并从研究人员那里获得口碑。

speaker: Unknown
en: But I think it also meant that our customers were people who really understood data and really cared about it.
zh: 但我也认为这意味着我们的客户是那些真正理解数据并且真正关心数据的人。

speaker: Unknown
en: I always thought it was really important for us to have early customers who were really aligned with what we were building, and who really cared about having really high quality data, and really understood how that data would make their AI models so much better because they were the ones helping us.
zh: 我总是认为我们要拥有真正与我们正在构建的东西保持一致、并且真正关心拥有高质量数据的早期客户，以及真正理解这些数据如何使他们的 AI 模型变得如此之好，因为是他们帮助我们，这一点非常重要。

speaker: Unknown
en: They were the ones giving us feedback on what we're producing.
zh: 他们是那些就我们生产的东西给我们反馈的人。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so just having that kind of very close mission alignment with our customers actually helped us early on.
zh: 所以，仅仅与我们的客户保持这种非常密切的使命一致性，实际上在早期帮助了我们。

speaker: Unknown
en: So these are people who basically just buying our product because they knew how different it was and because it was helping them rather than because they saw something in that current [inaudible 00:08:52].
zh: 所以这些人基本上只是购买我们的产品，因为他们知道它有多么不同，因为它在帮助他们，而不是因为他们在当前的[听不清 00:08:52]中看到了什么。

speaker: Unknown
en: So it made things harder for us, but I think in a really good way.
zh: 所以这让事情对我们来说变得更难了，但我认为是以一种非常好的方式。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's such an empowering story to hear this journey for founders that they don't need to be on Twitter all day promoting what they're doing.
zh: 听到这个旅程对于创始人来说是一个如此令人鼓舞的故事，他们不需要整天在 Twitter 上推广他们正在做的事情。

speaker: Unknown
en: They don't have to raise money.
zh: 他们不需要去融资。

speaker: Unknown
en: They can just kind of go heads down and build, so I love so much about the story of Surge.
zh: 他们可以就这样埋头苦干和构建，所以我非常喜欢 Surge 的故事。

speaker: Unknown
en: For people that don't know what Surge does, just to give us a quick explanation of what Surge is.
zh: 对于那些不知道 Surge 做什么的人，只是给我们一个关于 Surge 是什么的快速解释。

speaker: Unknown
en: We essentially teach AI models what's good and what's bad.
zh: 我们基本上是教 AI 模型什么是好的，什么是坏的。

speaker: Unknown
en: So we train them using human data, and there's a lot of different products that we have, like SFT, RHF, rubrics, verifiers, RL environments, and so on and so on, and then we also measure how well they're progressing.
zh: 所以我们使用人类数据来训练它们，我们有非常多的不同产品，像 SFT、RHF、rubrics、verifiers、RL 环境等等等等，然后我们还要衡量它们进展得如何。

speaker: Unknown
en: So essentially we're a data company.
zh: 所以基本上我们是一家数据公司。

speaker: Unknown
en: What you always talk about is the quality has been the big reason you guys have been so successful, the quality of the data.
zh: 你们经常谈论的是质量是你们如此成功的重要原因，数据的质量。

speaker: Unknown
en: What does it take to create higher quality data?
zh: 创造更高质量的数据需要什么？

speaker: Unknown
en: What do you all do differently?
zh: 你们有哪些不同的做法？

speaker: Unknown
en: What are people missing?
zh: 人们忽略了什么？

speaker: Unknown
en: I think most people don't understand what quality even means in this space.
zh: 我认为大多数人并不理解质量在这个领域里意味着什么。

speaker: Unknown
en: They think you could just throw bodies at a problem and get good data and that's completely wrong.
zh: 他们认为你可以只是把人力扔向问题并获得好的数据，但这完全是错的。

speaker: Unknown
en: Let me give you an example.
zh: 我给你举个例子。

speaker: Unknown
en: So imagine you wanted to train a model to write an eight line poem about the moon.
zh: 想象一下你想训练一个模型写一首关于月亮的八行诗。

speaker: Unknown
en: What makes it a good, high-quality poem?
zh: 是什么让它成为一首好的、高质量的诗？

speaker: Unknown
en: If you don't think deeply about quality, you'll be like, "Is this a poem?
zh: 如果你不深入思考质量，你会说，“这是一首诗吗？

speaker: Unknown
en: Does it contain eight lines?
zh: 它包含八行吗？

speaker: Unknown
en: Does it contain the word, moon?"
zh: 它包含月亮这个词吗？”

speaker: Unknown
en: You check all of these boxes, and if so, sure.
zh: 你检查所有这些框，如果是，当然。

speaker: Unknown
en: Yeah, you say it's a great problem.
zh: 是的，你说这是一个很棒的问题。

speaker: Unknown
en: But that's completely different from what we want.
zh: 但这完全不同于我们想要的。

speaker: Unknown
en: We are looking for a Nobel Prize-winning poetry.
zh: 我们寻找的是诺贝尔奖获奖级别的诗歌。

speaker: Unknown
en: Is this poetry unique?
zh: 这首诗是独特的吗？

speaker: Unknown
en: Is it full of subtle imagery?
zh: 它充满微妙的意象吗？

speaker: Unknown
en: Does it surprise you and target your heart?
zh: 它让你惊讶并触动你的内心吗？

speaker: Unknown
en: Does it teach you something about the nature of moonlight?
zh: 它是否教会了你一些关于月光本质的东西？

speaker: Unknown
en: Does it playthrough emotions?
zh: 它会拨动情绪吗？

speaker: Unknown
en: And does it make you think?
zh: 它让你思考吗？

speaker: Unknown
en: That's what we are thinking about when we think about high quality poem.
zh: 这就是当我们思考高质量诗歌时我们在思考的东西。

speaker: Unknown
en: So it might be like a haiku about moonlight on water.
zh: 所以它可能像是一首关于水面上月光的俳句。

speaker: Unknown
en: It might use internal rhyme and meter.
zh: 它可能使用内部韵律和格律。

speaker: Unknown
en: There are a thousand ways to write a poem about the moon, and in each one, gives you all these different insights into language, and imagery, and human expression, and I think thinking about quality in this way is really hard, it's hard to measure.
zh: 有一千种写关于月亮的诗的方法，每一种都给你所有这些关于语言、意象和人类表达的不同洞察，我认为以这种方式思考质量真的很难，很难衡量。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's really subjective, and complex, and rich.
zh: 它真的很主观，复杂，丰富。

speaker: Unknown
en: And it sets a really high bar.
zh: 它设定了一个非常高的标准。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so we have to build all of this technology in order to measure it, like thousands of signals on all of our workers, thousands of signals on every project, every task.
zh: 所以我们必须构建所有这些技术来衡量它，比如我们所有工作者身上的数千个信号，每个项目、每个任务上的数千个信号。

speaker: Unknown
en: We know at the end of the day, if you are good at writing poetry versus good at writing essays versus great at writing technical documentation.
zh: 我们最终知道，你是擅长写诗还是擅长写文章，还是擅长写技术文档。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so we have to gather all these signals on what your background is, what your expertise is, and not just that.
zh: 所以我们必须收集关于你的背景、你的专业的所有这些信号，不仅如此。

speaker: Unknown
en: Like how you're actually performing when you're writing all these things, and we use those signals to inform whether or not you are good [inaudible 00:11:23] for these projects, and whether or not you are improving the models.
zh: 就像你在写所有这些东西时实际表现如何，我们用这些信号来通知你是否适合这些项目，以及你是否在改进模型。

speaker: Unknown
en: And it's really hard, and so to build all this technology to measure it, but I think that's exactly what we want AI to do, and so we have these really deep notions about quality that we're always trying to try and achieve.
zh: 这真的很难，所以构建所有这些技术来衡量它，但我认为这正是我们想要 AI 去做的，所以我们有这些关于我们总是试图实现的质量的非常深刻的概念。

speaker: Unknown
en: So what I'm hearing is there's kind of just going much deeper in understanding what quality is within the verticals that you are selling data around.
zh: 所以我听到的是，在理解你们销售数据所在的垂直领域内什么是质量方面，只是要深入得多。

speaker: Unknown
en: And is this like a person you hire that is incredibly talented at poetry plus evals that they, I guess, help write, that tell them that this is great?
zh: 这是像你雇佣的一个在诗歌方面非常有才华的人，加上评估，我想，他们帮忙写的，告诉他们这很棒？

speaker: Unknown
en: What's the mechanics of that?
zh: 这背后的机制是什么？

speaker: Unknown
en: The way it works is we essentially gather thousands of signals about everything that you're doing when you're working on platform.
zh: 它的运作方式是，我们基本上收集数千个关于你在平台上工作时所做的每件事情的信号。

speaker: Unknown
en: So we are looking at your keyboard strokes.
zh: 所以我们看你的键盘敲击。

speaker: Unknown
en: We are looking how fast you answer things.
zh: 我们看你回答事情有多快。

speaker: Unknown
en: We are using reviews, we are using code standards, we are using... We're training models ourselves all on the outputs that you create, and then we're seeing whether they improve the model's performance.
zh: 我们使用评论，我们使用代码标准，我们使用……我们自己训练模型，针对你创建的所有输出，然后我们看它们是否提高了模型的性能。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so in a very similar way to how Google search, like when Google search is trying to determine what is a good webpage, there's almost two aspects of it.
zh: 所以用一种非常相似的方式，就像当谷歌搜索试图确定什么是一个好的网页时，它几乎有两个方面。

speaker: Unknown
en: One is you want to remove all of the worst of the worst webpages.
zh: 一个是你想要去除所有最差的网页。

speaker: Unknown
en: So you want to remove all the spam, all the just low quality content, all the pages that don't load, and so it's almost like a content moderation problem.
zh: 所以你想要去除所有的垃圾邮件，所有的低质量内容，所有无法加载的页面，所以这几乎像是一个内容审核问题。

speaker: Unknown
en: You just want to remove the worst of the worst.
zh: 你只是想要去除最差中的最差。

speaker: Unknown
en: But then you also want to discover the best of the best.
zh: 但随后你也想要发现最好的中的最好。

speaker: Unknown
en: Okay, like this is the best webpage or just the best person for this job.
zh: 好的，就像这是最好的网页，或者只是这份工作的最佳人选。

speaker: Unknown
en: They are not just somebody who writes the equivalent of high school level poetry.
zh: 他们不只是写出相当于高中水平诗歌的人。

speaker: Unknown
en: Again, they're not just [inaudible 00:12:57] writing poetry that checks all these boxes, checks all of these explicit instructions, but rather, yeah, they're writing poetry that makes you emotional.
zh: 同样，他们不只是 [听不清 00:12:57] 写出符合所有这些框框，符合所有这些明确指示的诗歌，而是，是的，他们写出的诗歌让你感动。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so we have all these signals as well that, again, completely differently from moving the worst of the worst, we are finding the best of the best.
zh: 所以我们也有所有这些信号，同样，与去除最差中的最差完全不同，我们正在寻找最好的中的最好。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so we have all these signals...
zh: 所以我们有所有这些信号...

speaker: Unknown
en: Again, just like Google Search uses all these signals that feeds them into their ML algorithms and uses and predicts certain types of things, we do the same with all of our workers and all of our tasks in all of our projects.
zh: 同样，就像谷歌搜索使用所有这些信号，将它们输入到他们的机器学习算法中，并使用和预测特定类型的事物，我们也对我们的所有工人、所有任务和所有项目做同样的事情。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so it's almost like a complicated machine learning problem at the end of the day, and that's how it works.
zh: 所以归根结底，这几乎就像是一个复杂的机器学习问题，它的工作原理就是这样。

speaker: Unknown
en: That is incredibly interesting.
zh: 这非常有意思。

speaker: Unknown
en: I want to ask you about something I've been very curious about over the past couple years.
zh: 我想问你一些我在过去几年里一直很好奇的事情。

speaker: Unknown
en: If you look at Claude, it's been so much better at coding and at writing than any other model for so long.
zh: 如果你看看 Claude，它在编码和写作方面比其他任何模型都好得如此之久。

speaker: Unknown
en: And it's really surprising just how long it took other companies to catch up.
zh: 其他公司花了这么长时间才赶上，真得令人惊讶。

speaker: Unknown
en: Considering just how much economic value there is there, just like every AI coding product sat on top of Claude because it was so good Claude code and writing also.
zh: 考虑到那里有如此巨大的经济价值，就像每一个 AI 编码产品都是建立在 Claude 之上的，因为它是如此优秀，Claude 的代码和写作也是如此。

speaker: Unknown
en: What is it that made it so much better?
zh: 是什么让它如此优秀？

speaker: Unknown
en: Is it just the quality of the data they trained on or is there something else?
zh: 仅仅是他们训练数据的质量，还是还有其他原因？

speaker: Unknown
en: I think there are multiple parts to it.
zh: 我认为这有多个部分。

speaker: Unknown
en: So a big part of it certainly is the data.
zh: 所以很大一部分肯定是数据。

speaker: Unknown
en: I think people don't realize that there's almost like this infinite amount of choices that all the frontier labs are deciding between when they're choosing what data goes into their models.
zh: 我认为人们没有意识到，所有前沿实验室在选择进入模型的数据时，几乎都在做无限量的选择。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's like, okay, are you purely using human data?
zh: 就像是，好的，你是纯粹使用人类数据吗？

speaker: Unknown
en: Are you gathering the human data in X, Y, Z way?
zh: 你是以 X、Y、Z 的方式收集人类数据吗？

speaker: Unknown
en: When you are gathering the human data, what exactly are you asking the people who are creating it to create for you?
zh: 当你在收集人类数据时，你到底在要求创造它的人为你创造什么？

speaker: Unknown
en: For example, in the coding realm, maybe you care more about front end coding versus back end coding.
zh: 例如，在编码领域，也许你更关心前端编码而不是后端编码。

speaker: Unknown
en: Maybe when you're doing front end coding, you care a lot about the visual design of the front end applications that you're creating, or maybe you don't care about it so much and you care more about, I don't know, the deficiency of it or the pure correctness over that visual design.
zh: 也许当你做前端编码时，你非常关心你所创建的前端应用程序的视觉设计，或者也许你不太关心它，你更关心，我不知道，它的缺陷，或者是那种优于视觉设计的纯粹正确性。

speaker: Unknown
en: And then other questions like, okay, are you carrying [inaudible 00:14:49] how much synthetic data are we throwing into the mix?
zh: 然后还有其他问题，比如，好的，你是否在 [听不清 00:14:49] 我们在混合中投入了多少合成数据？

speaker: Unknown
en: How much do you care about these 20 different benchmarks?"
zh: 你有多关心这 20 个不同的基准测试？

speaker: Unknown
en: Some companies, they see these benchmarks and they're like, "Okay, for PR purposes, even though we don't think that these academic benchmarks matter all that much, maybe we just need to optimize for them anyways because our marketing team needs to show certain progress on certain standard evaluations that every other company talks about, and if we don't show good performance here, it's going to be bad for us even if ignoring these academic benchmarks makes us better at the real tasks."
zh: 有些公司看到这些基准测试，他们会说，“好的，出于公关目的，即使我们认为这些学术基准测试没那么重要，也许我们无论如何都需要针对它们进行优化，因为我们的营销团队需要在其他每个公司都在谈论的某些标准评估上展示一定的进展，如果我们不在这里展示良好的性能，这对我们来说会很糟糕，即使忽略这些学术基准测试能让我们在真实任务上做得更好。”

speaker: Unknown
en: Other companies are going to be principled and be like, "Okay, yeah, no, I don't care about marketing.
zh: 其他公司会很有原则，会说，“好的，是的，不，我不关心营销。

speaker: Unknown
en: I just care about how my model performs on these real world tasks at the end of the day, and so I'm going to optimize for that instead."
zh: 我只关心我的模型在最终的真实世界任务上的表现，所以我将针对那个进行优化。”

speaker: Unknown
en: And it's almost like there's a trade-off between all of these different things, and there's like a...
zh: 这几乎就像所有这些不同事情之间的一种权衡，这里有一个...

speaker: Unknown
en: One of the things I often think about is that there's a... It's almost like there's an art to post training.
zh: 我经常想的事情之一是，这有一个……这几乎就像后训练是一门艺术。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's not purely a science.
zh: 这不纯粹是一门科学。

speaker: Unknown
en: When you are deciding what kind of model you're trying to create and what it's good at, there's this notion of taste and sophistication, like, "Okay, do I think that these..."
zh: 当你在决定你试图创造什么类型的模型以及它擅长什么时，这里有品味和精致的概念，像是，“好的，我认为这些……”

speaker: Unknown
en: So going back to the example of how good the model is at visual design.
zh: 所以回到模型在视觉设计方面有多好的例子。

speaker: Unknown
en: I'm like, "Okay, maybe you have a different notion of visual design than what I do.
zh: 我会说，“好的，也许你对视觉设计的概念与我不同。

speaker: Unknown
en: Maybe you care more about minimalism, and you care more about, I don't know, 3D animations than I do.
zh: 也许你更关心极简主义，你更关心，我不知道，3D 动画，比我更关心。

speaker: Unknown
en: And maybe this other person prefers things that look a little bit more broke."
zh: 也许这另一个人更喜欢看起来有点破碎的东西。

speaker: Unknown
en: And there's all these notions of taste sophistication that you have to decide between when you're designing your post training mix, and so that matters as well.
zh: 当你在设计你的后训练组合时，你必须决定所有这些品味和精致的概念，所以这也很重要。

speaker: Unknown
en: So long story short, I think there's all these different factors, and certainly the data is a big part of it, but it's also like what is the objective function that you're trying to optimize your model towards?
zh: 长话短说，我认为这里有所有这些不同的因素，当然数据是很大的一部分，但也像是你试图将模型优化向的目标函数是什么？

speaker: Unknown
en: That is so interesting.
zh: 这太有意思了。

speaker: Unknown
en: The taste of the person leading this work will inform what data they ask for, what data they feed it.
zh: 领导这项工作的人的品味将影响他们要求什么数据，他们输入什么数据。

speaker: Unknown
en: But it's wild it shows the value of great data.
zh: 但这很疯狂，它展示了优质数据的价值。

speaker: Unknown
en: Anthropic got so much growth and win from essentially better data.
zh: Anthropic 从本质上更好的数据中获得了如此多的增长和胜利。

speaker: Unknown
en: Yeah, exactly.
zh: 是的，完全正确。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I could see why companies like yours are growing so fast.
zh: 我可以理解为什么像你们这样的公司增长得这么快。

speaker: Unknown
en: There's just so much... And that's just one vertical.
zh: 只有太多……而这只是一个垂直领域。

speaker: Unknown
en: That's just coding, and then there's probably a similar area for writing.
zh: 这只是编码，然后写作领域可能也有类似的领域。

speaker: Unknown
en: I love that it's... It's interesting that AI, it feels like this artificial computer binary thing, but it's like taste.
zh: 我喜欢它是……有趣的是 AI，感觉像是这种人工的计算机二进制的东西，但它像是品味。

speaker: Unknown
en: Human judgment is still such a key factor in these things being successful.
zh: 人类的判断力依然是这些东西取得成功的一个关键因素。

speaker: Unknown
en: Yep, exactly.
zh: 是的，确实。

speaker: Unknown
en: Again, going back to the example I said earlier, certain companies, if you ask them what is good poem, they will simply robotically check off all of these instructions on our list.
zh: 再次回到我之前说的例子，有些公司，如果你问它们什么是好诗，它们只会机械地勾选我们列表上的所有这些指令。

speaker: Unknown
en: But again, I don't think that makes for good poetry, so certain frontier labs, the ones with more taste in sophistication, they will realize that it doesn't reduce to this six set of checkboxes and they'll consider all of these kind of implicit, very subtle qualities instead, and I think that's what makes them better at this at the end of the day.
zh: 但同样，我不认为这能造就好的诗歌，所以某些前沿实验室，那些具有更高级品味的实验室，他们会意识到这不能简化为这六组勾选框，他们会考虑所有这些隐含的、非常微妙的品质，我认为这就是为什么他们最终在这方面做得更好的原因。

speaker: Unknown
en: You mentioned benchmarks.
zh: 你提到了基准测试。

speaker: Unknown
en: This is something a lot of people worry about is there's all these models that are always... Basically, it feels like every model is better than humans at every STEM field at this point, but to a regular person, it doesn't feel like these models are getting that much smarter constantly.
zh: 这是很多人担心的一个问题，有这么多模型，总是……基本上，感觉每个模型现在在每个 STEM 领域都比人类强，但对普通人来说，感觉这些模型并没有那样不断地变得聪明。

speaker: Unknown
en: What's your just sense of how much you trust benchmarks and just how correlated those are with actual AI advancements?
zh: 你对基准测试的信任度如何，以及它们与实际 AI 进步的相关性如何，你有什么感觉？

speaker: Unknown
en: Yeah, so I don't trust the benchmarks at all.
zh: 是的，所以我根本不信任这些基准测试。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I think that's for two reasons.
zh: 我认为这有两个原因。

speaker: Unknown
en: So one is I think a lot of people don't realize, even researchers within the community, they don't realize that the benchmarks themselves are often honestly just wrong.
zh: 首先是我认为很多人没有意识到，即使是社区内部的研究人员，他们也没有意识到基准测试本身往往老实说就是错的。

speaker: Unknown
en: They have wrong answers.
zh: 它们有错误的答案。

speaker: Unknown
en: They're full of all this kind of messiness and people trust... Long as for the popular ones, people have maybe realized this to some extent, but the vast majority just have all these flaws that people don't realize.
zh: 它们充满了各种各样的混乱，人们信任……对于那些流行的来说，人们可能在某种程度上意识到了这一点，但绝大多数都有人们没有意识到的这些缺陷。

speaker: Unknown
en: So that's one part of it.
zh: 这是其中一部分。

speaker: Unknown
en: And the other part of it is these benchmarks at the end of the day, they often have well-defined objective answers that make them very easy for models to hill-climb on in a way that's very different from the messiness and ambiguity of the real world.
zh: 另一部分是这些基准测试归根结底通常有明确定义的客观答案，这使得模型很容易以一种与现实世界的混乱和模棱两可非常不同的方式进行“爬山”优化。

speaker: Unknown
en: I think one thing that I often say is that it's kind of crazy that these models can win IMO gold medals, but they still have trouble parsing PDFs.
zh: 我想我经常说的一件事是，这些模型能获得国际数学奥林匹克竞赛金牌，但它们在解析 PDF 时仍然有困难，这有点疯狂。

speaker: Unknown
en: And that's because, yeah, even though IMO gold medals seem hard to the average person, yeah, they are hard at the end of the day.
zh: 这是因为，是的，尽管国际数学奥林匹克竞赛金牌对普通人来说似乎很难，是的，它们归根结底是很难。

speaker: Unknown
en: But they have this notion of objectivity that, okay, yeah, parsing a PDF sometimes doesn't have.
zh: 但它们具有这种客观性的概念，好的，是的，解析 PDF 有时候没有这种客观性。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so it's easier for the frontier labs to hill-climb on all of these than to solve all these mess ambiguous problems in the real world.
zh: 因此，前沿实验室在这些测试上进行“爬山”优化比解决现实世界中所有这些混乱的模棱两可的问题要容易。

speaker: Unknown
en: So I think there's a lack of direct correlation there.
zh: 所以我认为那里缺乏直接的相关性。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's so interesting the way you described it is hitting these benchmarks is kind of like a marketing piece.
zh: 你描述的方式很有趣，达到这些基准测试就像是一个营销噱头。

speaker: Unknown
en: When you launch, say Gemini 3 just launched, and it's like, cool.
zh: 当你发布时，比如说 Gemini 3 刚刚发布，就像是，酷。

speaker: Unknown
en: Number one with all these benchmarks.
zh: 在所有这些基准测试中排名第一。

speaker: Unknown
en: Is that what happens?
zh: 是那样发生的吗？

speaker: Unknown
en: They just kind of train their models to get good at these very specific things?

speaker: Unknown

speaker: Unknown
en: The other part of it though is it's like by optimizing for the benchmark instead of optimizing for the real world, you will just naturally climb on the benchmark and, yeah, it's basically another form of gaming it.
zh: 但另一部分是，通过为基准测试进行优化而不是为现实世界进行优化，你自然会在基准测试上进行“爬山”优化，是的，这基本上只是钻空子的另一种形式。

speaker: Unknown
en: Knowing that with that in mind, how do you get a sense of if we're heading towards AGI, how do you measure progress?
zh: 知道了这一点，你如何判断我们是否正走向通用人工智能，你如何衡量进步？

zh: 例如，我们所做的是，是的，我们会聘请人类标注员，我们会问他们，“好的，去对话模型。”

speaker: Unknown
en: And maybe you're having this conversation with the model across all of these different topics.
zh: 也许你正在就所有这些不同的话题与模型进行对话。

speaker: Unknown
en: So you are a Nobel Prize winning physicist.
zh: 所以你是一位诺贝尔奖获得者物理学家。

speaker: Unknown
en: So you go have a conversation about pushing different tier of your own research.
zh: 所以你去进行关于推动你自己的研究不同层次的对话。

speaker: Unknown
en: You are a teacher and you're trying to create lesson plans for your students, so go talk to the model about these things.
zh: 你是一名老师，你正在为你的学生制定课程计划，所以去和模型谈论这些事情。

speaker: Unknown
en: Or you're a coder and you're working at one of these big tech companies, and you have these problems every day, so go talk to the model and see how much it helps you.
zh: 或者你是一名程序员，在这些大科技公司工作，你每天都有这些问题，所以去和模型谈谈，看看它有多大帮助。

speaker: Unknown
en: And because or searchers or annotators, they are experts at the top of their fields, and they are not just giving your responses, they're actually working through the responses deeply themselves, they are... Yeah, they're going to evaluate the code that it write.
zh: 因为无论是研究人员还是标注员，他们都是各自领域的顶尖专家，他们不仅仅是在给出回答，他们实际上是在自己深入地思考这些回答，他们……是的，他们将评估它编写的代码。

speaker: Unknown
en: They're going to double check the physics equations that it writes.
zh: 他们将仔细检查它编写的物理方程。

speaker: Unknown
en: They're going to evaluate the models in a very deep way, so they're going to pay attention to accuracy and instruction following, all these things that casual users don't when you suddenly get a popup on your ChatGPT response asking you to compare these two different responses.
zh: 他们将以非常深入的方式评估模型，所以他们将关注准确性和指令遵循，所有这些普通用户不会做的事情，当你突然在 ChatGPT 的回答中看到一个弹窗要求你比较这两个不同的回答时。

speaker: Unknown
en: People like that, they're not evaluating models deeply, they're just vibing and picking whatever response looks flashiest or [inaudible 00:21:38] are looking closely at responses and evaluating them for all of these different dimensions, and so I think that's a much better approach than these benchmarks or these random outline AV tests.
zh: 像那样的人，他们没有深入评估模型，他们只是在凭感觉并选择看起来最炫酷的回答或……那些正在仔细查看回答并从所有这些不同维度评估它们的人，我认为这是一种比这些基准测试或那些随机概述的 AV 测试好得多的方法。

speaker: Unknown
en: Again, I love just how central humans continue to be in all this work that we're not totally done yet.
zh: 再次，我只是很喜欢人类在所有这些工作中继续如此核心这一点，我们还没有完全完成。

speaker: Unknown
en: Is there going to be a point where we don't need these people anymore, that AI is so smart that, "Okay, we're good.

speaker: Unknown
en: Okay, cool.

zh: 我认为人们没有意识到，从 80% 的性能提高到 90%，再到 99%，再到 99.9%，以此类推，这之间有很大的差距。

speaker: Unknown

speaker: Unknown

speaker: Unknown
en: They're skimming these responses for two seconds and picking whatever looks flashiest.

speaker: Unknown
en: So a model can hallucinate everything.

speaker: Unknown
en: It can completely hallucinate.

speaker: Unknown
en: And these LLM-reading users love it.

speaker: Unknown

speaker: Unknown
en: And the problem is, again, because all of these frontier labs, they kind of have to pay attention to PR because their sales team, when they're trying to sell to all these enterprise customers, those enterprise customers will say, "Oh, well, but your model's only number five on LLM Arena, so why should I buy it?"
zh: 问题在于，同样因为所有这些前沿实验室，他们某种程度上必须关注 PR，因为当他们的销售团队试图向所有这些企业客户推销时，那些企业客户会说，“噢，好吧，但你们的模型在 LLM Arena 上只排第五，那我为什么要买它呢？”

speaker: Unknown
en: They have to, in some sense, pay attention to these leaderboards, and so what their researchers [inaudible 00:24:47] tell us is like they'll say, "The only way I'm going to get promoted at the end of the year is if I climb this leaderboard, even though I know that climbing it is probably going to make my model worse and accuracy [inaudible 00:24:57] following."
zh: 他们在某种意义上必须关注这些排行榜，所以他们的研究人员 [听不清 00:24:47] 告诉我们的是，他们会说，“我在年底获得晋升的唯一途径就是攀登这个排行榜，尽管我知道攀登它可能会让我的模型变差，而且准确性 [听不清 00:24:57] 随之下降。”

speaker: Unknown
en: So I think there's all these negative incentives that are pushing work in the wrong direction.
zh: 所以我认为所有这些负面激励都在将工作推向错误的方向。

speaker: Unknown
en: I'm also worried about this trend towards optimizing AI for engagement.

speaker: Unknown

speaker: Unknown

en: What an amazing question," the easiest way to hook users is to tell them how amazing they are.

speaker: Unknown

speaker: Unknown
en: So I think I'm really worried that all of these negative ascendants are pushing AGI into the wrong direction.

en: So what I'm hearing is AGI is being slowed down by these, basically the wrong objective function, these labs paying attention to the wrong basically benchmarks and evals.

speaker: Unknown

zh: 同样，只是因为……就像在选择你的模型如何处理这个问题的方式之间存在岔路口一样，对于模型拥有的所有其他问题也是如此，你想要的行为类型将从根本上影响它。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's almost like in the same way that when Google builds a search engine, it's very different from how Facebook would build a search engine, which is very different from how Apple would build a search engine.
zh: 这几乎就像 Google 构建搜索引擎的方式与 Facebook 构建搜索引擎的方式非常不同，这与 Apple 构建搜索引擎的方式非常不同是一样的。

speaker: Unknown
en: They all have their own principles and values and things that they're trying to achieve in the world that shape all the products that they're going to build.
zh: 它们都有自己的原则和价值观以及它们试图在世界上实现的东西，这些东西塑造了它们将构建的所有产品。

speaker: Unknown
en: And in the same way, I think all the [inaudible 00:50:40] will start behaving very differently too.
zh: 同样，我认为所有的 [inaudible 00:50:40] 也将开始表现得非常不同。

speaker: Unknown
en: That is incredibly interesting.
zh: 这非常有趣。

speaker: Unknown
en: You already see that with Grok.
zh: 你已经在 Grok 上看到了这一点。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's got a very different personality and a very different approach to answering questions.
zh: 它有非常不同的个性和非常不同的回答问题的方法。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so what I'm hearing is you're going to see more of this differentiation.
zh: 所以我听到的是，你将看到更多这种差异化。

speaker: Unknown
en: Yep.
zh: 是的。

speaker: Unknown
en: Kind of another question along these lines, what do you think is most under-hyped in AI that you think maybe people aren't talking enough about that is really cool?
zh: 沿着这条线的另一个问题，你认为在 AI 中什么是最被低估的，你认为人们可能谈论得不够但真的很酷的东西是什么？

speaker: Unknown
en: And what do you think is over-hyped?
zh: 还有你认为什么是被过度炒作的？

speaker: Unknown
en: So I think one of the things that's under-hyped is the built-in products that all of the chatbots are going to start having.
zh: 所以我认为被低估的事情之一是所有聊天机器人都将开始拥有的内置产品。

speaker: Unknown
en: I've always been a huge fan of Claude's artifacts.
zh: 我一直是 Claude 的 artifacts 的超级粉丝。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I think it just works really well.
zh: 我认为它真的非常好用。

speaker: Unknown
en: And actually the other day, I don't know if it's a new feature or not, but it asked me to help me create an email, and then it just created... So it didn't quite work because it didn't allow me to send the email.
zh: 实际上前几天，我不知道这是否是一个新功能，但它请求我帮助它创建一封电子邮件，然后它就创建了……所以它不太行，因为它不允许我发送那封电子邮件。

speaker: Unknown
en: But what it created instead was like a little, I don't know what we call it, like a little box where I could click on it and it would just text someone that did this message.
zh: 但它创建的替代品就像一个小小的，我不知道我们叫它什么，像一个小盒子，我可以点击它，它就会给执行此消息的人发短信。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I think that concept of taking artifacts to the next level where you just have these mini apps, mini UIs within the chatbots themselves, I feel like people aren't talking enough about that.
zh: 我认为这个将 artifacts 提升到一个新水平的概念，你在聊天机器人内部拥有这些迷你应用程序、迷你 UI，我觉得人们对此谈论得不够。

speaker: Unknown
en: So I think that that's one under-hyped area.
zh: 所以我认为那是被低估的一个领域。

speaker: Unknown
en: And in terms of over-hyped areas, I definitely think that vibe coding is over-hyped.
zh: 就过度炒作的领域而言，我绝对认为 vibe coding 被过度炒作。

speaker: Unknown
en: I think people don't realize how much it's going to make your systems unmaintainable in the long-term and they simply dump this code into their code bases if this seems to work out right now, so I kind of worry about the future of coding.
zh: 我认为人们没有意识到它在长期内会让你的系统变得多么不可维护，如果现在看起来还行，他们就直接把这些代码倾倒到他们的代码库中，所以我有点担心编码的未来。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's just going to keep on happening.
zh: 它只会不断发生。

speaker: Unknown
en: These are amazing answers.
zh: 这些答案太棒了。

speaker: Unknown
en: On that first point, there's something I actually asked.
zh: 关于第一点，有一件事其实我问过。

speaker: Unknown
en: I have the chief product officer of Anthropic and OpenAI, Kevin Weil and Mike Krieger on the podcast, and I asked them just like, "As a product team, you have this gigabrain intelligence.
zh: 我请到了 Anthropic 和 OpenAI 的首席产品官，Kevin Weil 和 Mike Krieger 做客播客，我就问他们，「作为一个产品团队，你们拥有这种巨型大脑般的智能。

speaker: Unknown
en: How long do you even need product teams?"
zh: 你们甚至还需要产品团队多久？」

speaker: Unknown
en: You think this AI will just create the product for you.
zh: 你认为这个 AI 会直接为你创造产品。

speaker: Unknown
en: "Here's what I want."
zh: 「这就是我想要的。」

speaker: Unknown
en: It's like the next level of vibe coding.
zh: 这就像是 vibe coding 的下一个级别。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's just like tell it, "Here's what I want," and it's just building the product and involving the product as you're using it.
zh: 就像是告诉它，「这就是我想要的」，它就正在构建产品并在你使用它时涉及产品。

speaker: Unknown
en: And it feels like that's what you're describing is where we might be heading.
zh: 感觉这就像你正在描述的地方，可能是我们前进的方向。

speaker: Unknown
en: Yeah, I think there's a very powerful notion where it helps people just achieve their ideas in a much cooler way.
zh: 是的，我认为有一个非常强大的概念，它帮助人们以一种更酷的方式实现他们的想法。

speaker: Unknown
en: Something we haven't gotten into that I think is really interesting is just the story of how you got to starting Surge.
zh: 我们还没有深入讨论但我觉得非常有趣的一件事，就是你开始创立 Surge 的故事。

speaker: Unknown
en: You have a really unique background.
zh: 你有一个非常独特的背景。

speaker: Unknown
en: I always think about these... Brian Armstrong, the founder of Coinbase, once gave this talk that has really stuck with me where he kind of talked about how his very unique background allowed him to start Coinbase.
zh: 我总是想到这些……Coinbase 的创始人 Brian Armstrong 曾经发表过一个演讲，给我留下了很深的印象，他谈到他非常独特的背景如何让他能够创立 Coinbase。

speaker: Unknown
en: He had a economics background, he had a cryptography experience, and then he was an engineer.
zh: 他有经济学背景，他有密码学经验，然后他是一个工程师。

speaker: Unknown
en: And it's like the perfect Venn diagram for starting Coinbase, and I feel like you have a very similar story with Surge.
zh: 这就像创立 Coinbase 的完美韦恩图，我觉得你有着和 Surge 非常相似的故事。

speaker: Unknown
en: Talk about that, your background there, and how that led to Surge.
zh: 谈谈那个，你在那里的背景，以及那是如何导致 Surge 的。

speaker: Unknown
en: Going way back, I was always fascinated by math and language when I was a kid.
zh: 早在很久以前，当我还是个孩子的时候，我总是对数学和语言着迷。

speaker: Unknown
en: I went to MIT because it's obviously one of the best places for math and CS, but also because it's the home of Noam Chomsky.
zh: 我去了 MIT，因为它显然是数学和 CS 最好的地方之一，但也因为它是 Noam Chomsky 的故乡。

speaker: Unknown
en: My dream in school was actually to find some underlying theory connecting all these different fields.
zh: 我在学校的梦想实际上是找到一个连接所有这些不同领域的基础理论。

speaker: Unknown
en: And then I became a researcher at Google, and Facebook, and Twitter, and I just kept running into the same problem over and over again.
zh: 然后我成为了 Google、Facebook 和 Twitter 的研究员，我总是反复遇到同样的问题。

speaker: Unknown
en: It was impossible to get the data that we needed to train our models.
zh: 不可能获得我们需要来训练我们模型的数据。

speaker: Unknown
en: So I was always this huge believer in the need for high quality data, and then GPT-3 came out in 2020.
zh: 所以我一直坚信需要高质量数据，然后 GPT-3 在 2020 年出现了。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I realized that, yeah, if we wanted to take things to the next level and build models that could code, and use tools, and tell jokes, and write poetry, and solve [inaudible 00:54:12], and cure cancer, then yeah, we were going to need a completely new solution.
zh: 我意识到，是的，如果我们想把事情提升到一个新的水平，并构建能够编码、使用工具、讲笑话、写诗歌、解决问题和治愈癌症的模型，那么是的，我们将需要一个全新的解决方案。

speaker: Unknown
en: The thing that always drove me crazy when I was at all these companies was we had a full power of the human mind in front of us, and all the data students out there were focused on really simple things like image labeling.
zh: 当我在所有这些公司时，一直让我发疯的事情是，我们面前有人类思维的全部力量，而外面的所有数据学生都专注于像图像标注这样非常简单的事情。

speaker: Unknown
en: So I wanted to build something focus on all these advanced, complex use cases instead that would really help us build our next generation models.
zh: 所以我想构建一些专注于所有这些高级、复杂用例的东西，这真的会帮助我们构建我们的下一代模型。

speaker: Unknown
en: So yeah, I think my background in kind of across math, and computer science, and linguistics really informed what I always wanted to do, and so I started Surge a month later with our one mission to basically build the use cases that I thought were going to be needed to push the frontier of AI.
zh: 所以是的，我认为我在数学、计算机科学和语言学方面的背景真的告知了我一直想做的事情，所以我在一个月后与我们的一个使命一起创立了 Surge，基本上就是构建我认为推进 AI 前沿所需要的用例。

speaker: Unknown
en: And you said a month later, a month later after what?
zh: 你说一个月后，一个月后在什么之后？

speaker: Unknown
en: After a GPT-3 launch in 2020.
zh: 在 GPT-3 于 2020 年发布之后。

speaker: Unknown
en: Oh, okay.
zh: 哦，好的。

speaker: Unknown
en: Wow.
zh: 哇。

speaker: Unknown
en: Okay.
zh: 好的。

speaker: Unknown
en: Yeah.
zh: 是的。

speaker: Unknown
en: A great decision.
zh: 一个很棒的决定。

speaker: Unknown
en: What just kind of drives you at this point of... Other than just the epic success you're having, what keeps you motivated to keep building this and building something in this space?
zh: 在这个时候是什么驱动你……除了你正在拥有的史诗般的成功，是什么让你保持动力去继续构建这个并在这个空间构建东西？

speaker: Unknown
en: I think I'm a scientist at heart.
zh: 我认为我本质上是一个科学家。

speaker: Unknown
en: I always thought I was going to become this math or CS professor and work on trying to understand the universe, and language, and the nature of communication.
zh: 我总是以为我会成为一名数学或 CS 教授，致力于试图理解宇宙、语言和沟通的本质。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's kind of funny, but I always had this fanciful dream where if aliens ever came to visit Earth and we need to figure out how to communicate with them, I wanted to be the one the government would call.
zh: 这有点好笑，但我总是有一个异想天开的梦想，如果外星人曾经来访问地球，我们需要弄清楚如何与他们交流，我想成为政府会打电话的那个人。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I'd use all this fancy math, and computer science, and linguistics to decipher it.
zh: 我会用所有这些花哨的数学、计算机科学和语言学来破译它。

speaker: Unknown
en: So even today, what I love doing most is every time a new model is released, we'll actually do a really deep dive into the model itself.
zh: 所以即使在今天，我最喜欢做的事情仍然是每次有新模型发布时，我们实际上会对模型本身进行一次非常深入的挖掘。

speaker: Unknown
en: I'll play around with it, I'll run evals, I'll compare where it's improved, where it's arrest, I'll create this really deep dive analysis that we send our customers.
zh: 我会摆弄它，我会运行评估，我会比较它哪里改进了，哪里停滞不前，我会创建这种我们发送给客户的真正深入的分析。

speaker: Unknown
en: And it's actually kind of funny because a lot of times we'll say it's from a data science team, but often it's actually just from me.
zh: 这其实有点好笑，因为很多时候我们会说这是来自数据科学团队，但通常实际上只是我做的。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I think I could do this all day.
zh: 我想我可以整天做这个。

speaker: Unknown
en: I have a very hard time being in meetings all day.
zh: 我很难整天都在开会。

speaker: Unknown
en: I'm terrible at sales, I'm terrible at doing the typical CEO things that people expect you to do, but I love writing these analyses.
zh: 我非常不擅长销售，也不擅长做人们期望你做的典型 CEO 的事情，但我喜欢写这些分析。

speaker: Unknown
en: I love jamming with our research team about what we're seeing, sometimes I'll be up until 3:00 AM just talking on the phone with somebody on the research team and [inaudible 00:56:12] model.
zh: 我喜欢和我们的研究团队一起探讨我们所看到的东西，有时我会一直待到凌晨 3 点，只是和研究团队的人在电话里交谈，[听不清 00:56:12] 模型。

speaker: Unknown
en: So I love that I still get to be really hands-on, working on the data and the science all day.
zh: 所以我仍然很喜欢能够非常亲力亲为地整天处理数据和科学工作。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I think what drives me is that I want Surge to play this critical role in the future of AI, which I think is also the future of humanity.
zh: 我想驱动我的动力是，我希望 Surge 在 AI 的未来中发挥这种关键作用，我认为这也是人类的未来。

speaker: Unknown
en: We have these really unique perspectives on data, and language, and quality, and how to measure all of this, and how to ensure it's all going on the right path.
zh: 我们对这些非常独特的看法，关于数据、语言、质量，以及如何衡量这一切，以及如何确保这一切都走在正确的道路上。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I think we're uniquely unconstrained by all of these influences that can sometimes steer companies in a negative direction.
zh: 我认为我们独特地不受所有这些有时会将公司引向负面方向的影响所约束。

speaker: Unknown
en: Like what I was saying earlier, we built Surge a lot more like a research lab than a typical startup.
zh: 就像我之前说的，我们在很大程度上把 Surge 建得更像一个研究实验室，而不是一个典型的初创公司。

speaker: Unknown
en: So we care about curiosity and long-term incentives and intellectual rigor, and we don't care as much about quarterly metrics and what's going to look good in a [inaudible 00:56:56].
zh: 所以我们关心好奇心、长期激励和智力严谨性，而不太关心季度指标以及什么在 [听不清 00:56:56] 中看起来很好。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so my goal is to take all these unique things about us as a company and use that to make sure that we're shaping AI in a way that's really beneficial for our species in the long term.
zh: 所以我的目标是利用我们作为一家公司的所有这些独特之处，以此来确保我们正在以一种从长远来看真正造福于我们物种的方式塑造 AI。

speaker: Unknown
en: What I'm realizing in this conversation is just how much influence you have and companies like yours have on where AI heads.
zh: 我在这段对话中意识到的是，你以及像你这样的公司对 AI 的发展方向有多么大的影响。

speaker: Unknown
en: The fact that you help labs understand where they have gaps and where they need to improve, and it's not just everyone looks at just like the heads of OpenAI and Anthropic and all these companies as they're the ones ushering in AI, but what I'm hearing here is you have a lot of influence on where things head too.
zh: 事实是你帮助实验室了解他们的差距在哪里以及他们需要在哪里改进，而且不仅仅是每个人都只看着 OpenAI 和 Anthropic 以及所有这些公司的负责人，认为他们是引入 AI 的人，但我在这里听到的是，你对我们事物的发展方向也有很大的影响力。

speaker: Unknown
en: Yeah, I think there's this really powerful ecosystem where, honestly, people just don't know where models are headed and how they want to shape them yet and how they want humanity kind of like [inaudible 00:57:47] play a role in the future of all of this, and so I think there's a lot of opportunity to just continue shaping the discussion.
zh: 是的，我认为有一个非常强大的生态系统，老实说，人们只是不知道模型的发展方向，他们想要如何塑造它们，以及他们想要人类如何 [听不清 00:57:47] 在所有这些的未来中发挥作用，所以我认为有很多机会可以继续塑造这个讨论。

speaker: Unknown
en: Along that thread, I know you have a very strong thesis on just why this work matters to humanity and why this is so important, talk about that.
zh: 沿着这个线索，我知道你有一个非常强烈的论点，关于为什么这项工作对人类很重要以及为什么这如此重要，谈谈这个吧。

speaker: Unknown
en: I'll get a bit philosophical here, but I think the question itself is a bit philosophical, so bear with me.
zh: 我在这里会有点哲学化，但我认为这个问题本身有点哲学化，所以请多包涵。

speaker: Unknown
en: So the most straightforward way of thinking about what we do is we train and evaluate AI.
zh: 所以思考我们所做事情最直接的方式是我们训练和评估 AI。

speaker: Unknown
en: But there's a deeper mission that I often think about, which is helping our customers think about their dream objective functions.
zh: 但我经常思考有一个更深的使命，那就是帮助我们的客户思考他们的梦想目标函数。

speaker: Unknown
en: Like yeah, what kind of model do they want their model to be?
zh: 就像，他们想要他们的模型成为什么样的模型？

speaker: Unknown
en: And once we help them do that, we'll help them train their model to reach their north star and we'll help them measure that progress.
zh: 一旦我们帮助他们做到这一点，我们将帮助他们的模型训练以达到他们的北极星，我们将帮助衡量那个进步。

speaker: Unknown
en: But it's really hard because objective functions are really rich and complex.
zh: 但这真的很难，因为目标函数真的很丰富和复杂。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's kind of like the difference between having a kid and asking them, "Okay, what test do you want to pass?
zh: 这有点像拥有一个孩子和问他们之间的区别，“好的，你想通过什么考试？”

speaker: Unknown
en: Do you want them to get a high score on SAT and write a really good college essay?"
zh: 你希望他们在 SAT 上取得高分并写出一篇非常好的大学论文吗？

speaker: Unknown
en: That's a simplistic version versus what kind of person do you want them to grow up to be?
zh: 这是一个简单的版本，对比你希望他们长成什么样的人？

speaker: Unknown
en: Will you be happy if they're happy no matter what they do or are you hoping they'll go to a good school and be financially successful?
zh: 如果他们快乐，无论他们做什么你都会快乐，还是你希望他们上一所好学校并在经济上取得成功？

speaker: Unknown
en: And again, if you take that notion, it's like, okay, how do you define happiness?
zh: 同样，如果你采纳那个概念，就像，好的，你如何定义幸福？

speaker: Unknown
en: How do you measure whether they're happy?
zh: 你如何衡量他们是否快乐？

speaker: Unknown
en: How do you measure whether they're financially successful?
zh: 你如何衡量他们是否在经济上取得成功？

speaker: Unknown
en: It's a lot harder than something measuring whether or not you're getting a high score on the SAT, and what we're doing is we want to help our customers reach, again, their dream north stars and figure out how to measure them.
zh: 这比衡量你是否在 SAT 上获得高分要难得多，我们所做的是我们想帮助我们的客户达到，再次强调，他们梦想的北极星，并找出如何衡量它们。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so I talked about this example of what you want models to do when you're asking them to write 50 different evaluations.
zh: 所以我谈到了这个例子，当你要求模型编写 50 个不同的评估时，你想要模型做什么。

speaker: Unknown
en: Do you just continue them for 50 more or do you just say, "No, just move on [inaudible 00:59:25] because this is perfect enough."
zh: 你只是让他们再继续做 50 个，还是只是说，“不，直接继续 [听不清 00:59:25] 因为这已经足够完美了。”

speaker: Unknown
en: And the broader question is, are we building these systems that actually advance humanity?
zh: 更广泛的问题是，我们正在构建这些真正推动人类进步的系统吗？

speaker: Unknown
en: And if so how do we build the data sets to train towards that and measure it?
zh: 如果是这样，我们要如何构建数据集来朝那个方向训练并衡量它？

speaker: Unknown
en: Are we optimizing for all of these wrong things, just systems that suck up more and more of our time and make us lazier and lazier?
zh: 我们是在为所有这些错误的事情做优化吗，只是那些吸走我们越来越多时间并让我们变得越来越懒惰的系统？

speaker: Unknown
en: And yeah, I think it's really relevant to what we do because it's very hard and difficult to measure and define whether something is genuinely advancing humanity.
zh: 是的，我认为这与我们所做的非常相关，因为衡量和定义某事是否真正推动人类进步是非常困难和艰巨的。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's very easy to measure all these proxies instead like clicks and likes.
zh: 衡量所有这些代理指标（比如点击和点赞）却很容易。

speaker: Unknown
en: But I think that's why our work is so interesting.
zh: 但我认为这就是为什么我们的工作如此有趣。

speaker: Unknown
en: We want to work the hard, important metrics that require the hardest types of data and not just the easy ones.
